forbidden_phrases: I hope this message finds you well
I hustled hard and it paid off

required_style.capitalization: lowercase default — no mid-sentence caps unless proper name
required_style.sentence_length: short — 2-4 lines ideally, builds slowly then lands with data or twist
required_style.ellipsis: use for trailing thoughts and natural pauses
required_style.periods: skip period on last sentence sometimes — real people do this
required_style.casual_openers: hey, so, yeah, btw — occasionally, not every time
required_style.casual_abbreviations: rn, tbh, ngl, btw — occasionally, naturally
required_style.x_articles_length: 2000+ words minimum, essay depth, short paragraphs per section

tone_guardrails.bullet_points_in_dms: reject — write in sentences like a human
tone_guardrails.soft_hooks: caution — observation posts reach people but don't move them
tone_guardrails.closed_satire: caution — satirical list format needs open loop/question at end

hooks: i worked on transformers before GPT existed. i did MLOps before MLOps was a word. (Instagram carousel — VIRAL: 1,200 comments, 20K views, +700 followers)
your company didn't lay you off because of AI. they used AI as the excuse. (LinkedIn — 24 comments, 17 reposts)
entry-level tech jobs are dying. senior tech jobs are fine. nobody's explaining why the gap exists. (X thread — biggest impression day of the week)

formats_by_platform.instagram: carousel: authority stacking + insider knowledge + Comment X CTA → VIRAL
formats_by_platform.linkedin: false consensus → data flip → counter-narrative → p.s with resource
formats_by_platform.x: numbered thread (1/11 through 11/11), single idea per tweet, closing tweet that stands alone

emotional_archetypes: contrarian take backed by real data
tension between what people believe and what's actually true
early-career Indian tech worker fear + ambition
viral_structures: I [did thing] before [thing] existed — authority stacking
[False statement most people believe]. [Real data that contradicts it]. — debate-starter
X is dying. Y is fine. Nobody's explaining why the gap exists. — X threads

voice_lessons: Instagram CTAs must use 'Comment X' format. No exceptions. 20x engagement gap vs passive CTAs.
Optimize for reposts on LinkedIn, not impressions. Reposts = people stamping their name on your take.
Specific data makes posts shareable. BLS stats, exact numbers, named companies get reposts. Observation posts get likes.
Personal authority > news commentary. Insider POV outperforms journalist POV.
Satirical list format needs an open loop. End with question or 'who's next?' to force response.
Comments-to-impressions ratio > likes-to-impressions ratio. 14K impressions + 24 comments beats 18K + 1 comment.
Target audience: 90.6% India, 87.8% male, 46.7% aged 18-24. Early-career Indian tech workers. Fear + ambition.
Peak engagement: Friday-Saturday, noon and evening IST.

AYUSH'S ACTUAL WRITING PATTERNS (extracted from hand-written post, 2026-03-16): Uses 'etc…' naturally. Uses 'initially' as temporal opener. Keeps trailing '…' before a 'but' to create tension before a reveal. The pattern: setup problem → 'but there's one thing which I did which changed the whole scenario' → outcome → insight → CTA. Uses 'and much more' casually instead of exhaustive lists. Slightly relaxed grammar ('which changed the whole scenario where I got back to back offers') — sounds like a person talking, not writing. 'Visibility' can be capitalized when used as the final punch word. 'ai' lowercase except when used as the subject of a punchy closer line.

AYUSH LINKEDIN STRUCTURE — Each post must have a completely different structure, not just a different hook. Confirmed structures from 3 reference posts: (1) principle-first → brief personal mention → insight → tactic → CTA. (2) contrast observation → name the problem people get stuck on → specific practical exercise with exact steps → CTA. (3) chronological story with specific year/date → the exact moment things changed → result with specifics → the principle behind why it worked → CTA. CTA keyword rotates every post and relates to the content (video / content / OFFER / HIRED etc). Caps on the CTA keyword sometimes (comment OFFER, comment HIRED) but lowercase on casual ones (comment video, comment content). Never repeat the same structure twice in a series.

AYUSH LINKEDIN — WHAT MAKES POSTS UNIQUE: It is not enough to change the hook. The entire post structure must be different. Post 1 used the AI moat angle. Post 2 used contrast observation + gave a specific exercise (therapist prompt). Post 3 told a chronological personal story with a specific year. Every post needs a different way IN to the same pillar topic.

CRITICAL AI TELL TO AVOID — The staccato pattern: 'X sentence. Y sentence. Z sentence.' where every thought is its own isolated one-liner is one of the clearest AI signals on LinkedIn. Real writing connects thoughts. Sentences flow into each other. They don't all end abruptly at the same rhythm. Ayush's actual writing mixes sentence lengths and lets ideas run into each other naturally — commas, 'etc…', 'and much more', 'but' — not full stops after every fragment.

AYUSH LANGUAGE PATTERNS (from reference posts): Uses 'cos' instead of 'because'. Uses 'you know?' as a casual conversational aside. Uses 'absolute ZERO' style CAPS for single-word emphasis — not for decorating, only when he genuinely wants to punch a word. Uses 'etc…' as trailing connector. Uses 'and i get what i didn't get back then' style callbacks. Contractions everywhere. Very natural, sounds like he's talking to a friend.

AYUSH CTA FORMATS — Two types: (1) Comment trigger: 'comment VIDEO/CONTENT/OFFER/HIRED below. i made a loom on X and i'll DM it to you.' — used when offering a specific resource via DM. (2) p.s. format: 'p.s. i share a lot of these insights and tips on X, community link is in first comment.' — used when pointing to a community/broader resource. Never mix these up. The comment trigger CTA keyword is sometimes lowercase (video, content) and sometimes caps (OFFER, HIRED) depending on the post tone.

AYUSH POST 4 STRUCTURE (reference 2026-03-16): Hook = 3-line contrast setup (founders can't find / engineers can't get / i've been on both sides). Then: personal credibility establishing shot. Then: what I see from the hiring side now. Then: the AI angle woven in mid-post naturally, not as the main point. Then: the principle/reframe. Then: the sharp closer ('the job market isn't broken. the matching is.'). Then: p.s. CTA. The reframe line is the most shareable part of the post.

AYUSH POST 5 STRUCTURE (reference 2026-03-16): Hook = specific timeframe + curiosity gap ('5 years ago... one thing got me hired. it wasn't my skills.'). Then: the story of what he actually did (went back to study, rebuilt). Then: BUT — that still wasn't what changed things. Then: the real insight delivered as a reframe (treating companies like clients). Then: the analogy that makes the insight click (companies spend money to get monthly customers → you ARE a monthly subscription → most people do ZERO marketing on themselves). Then: why this explains unemployment right now. Then: p.s. CTA. The analogy is the engine of the post.

linkedin_lp4_final.title: Founders can't find engineers. Engineers can't get jobs.
linkedin_lp4_final.content:
founders can't find engineers. engineers can't get jobs. i've been on both sides.
when i was looking for work, i thought the market was broken. smart people going unnoticed, good work getting ignored. i was one of them.
now i run SBL where we help US businesses get customers through linkedin outbound at scale.
and i get what i didn't get back then.
when we need to hire someone, we're not opening Naukri and scrolling through 300 applications. we look for people who've been showing up in our feed, who've posted about the kind of problem we're trying to solve, who've built something close to what we need.
the person i want to hire is someone who can learn fast and is actually visible. cos ai can do almost everything now. the only real moat left is how you use ai to be better than the next person who's also using ai. you know?
this isn't companies being unfair. it's just how attention works. if you don't show up anywhere, nobody can find you. doesn't matter how good you are.
the engineers i most want to hire right now are the ones whose names i already recognise. not the most qualified on paper. the ones who've been in my feed.
if you're an engineer who thinks posting online is beneath you, you're probably invisible to the exact founders who'd love to hire you.
the job market isn't broken. the matching is. two groups looking in completely different places.
p.s. i share a lot of these insights and tips on a modern hiring roadmap for developers/engineers, community link is in first comment.
linkedin_lp4_final.notes: Ayush's own version. p.s. CTA format. 'cos' language. 'you know?' aside. Reframe closer: 'the job market isn't broken. the matching is.'

linkedin_lp5_final.title: 5 years ago one thing got me hired. it wasn't my skills.
linkedin_lp5_final.content:
5 years ago when i was job hunting, one thing got me hired. it wasn't my skills.
i'd just been destroyed in an interview. the interviewer exposed every gap i had. i went back and studied class 9-12 maths from scratch, re-learned probability, linear algebra, calculus. took me months.
but that still wasn't what changed things.
what changed things was treating companies like clients instead of treating them like people who owe me a job.
think about it this way. businesses pour a huge amount of money into sales and marketing just to sell their product at x USD per month. they run ads, do outreach, build funnels, all of it just to get someone to pay them monthly.
now here's the thing. when a company hires you, they're also paying you monthly. you're basically a product on a monthly subscription. but most people do absolute ZERO effort on marketing and distributing themselves. no outreach, no presence, no nothing.
and that's one of the biggest reasons a lot of people are unemployed right now. they're technically fine at what they do, but they're not putting themselves out there at all. nobody knows they exist.
p.s. i share a lot of these insights and tips on a modern hiring roadmap for developers/engineers, community link is in first comment.
linkedin_lp5_final.notes: Ayush's own version. Specific timeframe hook + curiosity gap. BUT pivot structure. Subscription analogy is the engine. ZERO in caps for emphasis. p.s. CTA.

linkedin_lp1_final.title: Visibility is the only thing AI can't replace
linkedin_lp1_final.content:
claude can write better code than you right now.

it can review your PRs, build project plans and much more. 

there's one thing it can't do.

show up in someone's feed.

initially i was sending applications into portals, applying through several job listing sites, etc… 

but there's one thing which I did which changed the whole scenario where I got back to back offers from several US based firms. 

and here's what I understood - companies don't hire whoever applies. they hire whoever's already in their head.

everyone says "be visible." but nobody teaches the actual mode. how to show up in the feed consistently enough that when a problem comes up, your name is already there.

the world is smaller than you think. write one post per day about what you're building. it will reach someone who knows someone who's hiring.

you don't need to go viral. you need to be in the feed.

ai took the code, reviews, debugging… but 

Visibility is the only thing it can't replace. 

comment "video" below. i made a loom on how to build this system as a student or engineer and i'll DM it to you.
linkedin_lp1_final.notes: This is Ayush's own hand-written version. Use as the gold standard for LinkedIn voice calibration.

feedback_log.what_was_wrong: LP-2 drafted Ayush as someone who was invisible early in his career. That's factually wrong — he was visible, which is how he got jobs and opportunities. The post invented a narrative that contradicted his real story.
feedback_log.what_fixed_it: Flipped to an observer angle — Ayush has watched students with excellent technical skills get zero opportunities while average engineers with visibility landed back to back US offers. Grounded in his real experience running courses and placing hundreds of students.
feedback_log.rule_added: Never invent a personal failure narrative for Ayush unless he explicitly provides it. When his personal story doesn't fit the angle, use the observer/witness framing instead.
